message: What is RAG and how does retrieval work?
answer:
RAG, or Retrieval-Augmented Generation, is a technique that enhances a language model by grounding it in external knowledge. Instead of relying solely on what the model learned during training, RAG retrieves relevant documents at the time of a query and includes them as context in the prompt. This helps reduce hallucinations and allows the model to answer questions about private or recent information it hasn't seen before.

The RAG process has two main phases:

1. Offline ingestion: Source documents are loaded, split into chunks, and each chunk is converted into an embedding vector. These vectors are stored in a vector database.
2. Online retrieval: When a user submits a query, it is embedded using the same model, and a nearest-neighbor search finds the most similar document chunks. The top matches are then added to the prompt sent to the language model.

This retrieval mechanism enables the language model to dynamically access up-to-date and relevant information, improving the accuracy and relevance of its responses.

data.query: What is RAG and how does retrieval work?
data.plan: Decide which tools to call — search the knowledge base, run a calculation, check the time — then answer.

tools.name: search_knowledge_base
tools.description: Search the curated AI-engineering documentation corpus (vector RAG). It covers ONLY these topics: large language models and tokens, embeddings and vector search, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), AI agents and the ReAct loop, the Model Context Protocol (MCP), and prompt engineering — plus any documents the user has uploaded. Use it to ground answers about those subjects. Do NOT use it for real-world facts, current events, news, sports, people, prices, or anything outside AI engineering — for those, use `web_search`. This corpus always returns its closest passages even when nothing is truly relevant, so only call it on-topic.

tools.name: calculator
tools.description: Evaluate a basic arithmetic expression, e.g. '2 + 2' or '12 * (3 + 1)'.

tools.name: current_time
tools.description: Return the current date and time in UTC.

tools.name: kb_lookup
tools.description: Look up a one-line glossary definition for an AI engineering topic.

tools.name: load_skill
tools.description:
Load the full instructions (body) of a named skill from the catalog.

    Call this with the exact `name` of a skill from the advertised skills list when
    it fits the user's request, then follow the loaded instructions.

tools.name: web_search
tools.description:
Search the live internet and return a synthesized answer plus top sources.

    This is the right tool for ANY question outside AI engineering — real-world
    facts, current events, news, sports, people, prices, specific documentation.
    Prefer it over `search_knowledge_base` whenever the question is not about LLMs,
    embeddings, RAG, agents, MCP, or prompting. Pass a focused natural-language
    `query`.

tools.name: write_todos
tools.description: Record or update an ordered todo list for a multi-step task. Call this FIRST, before acting, whenever the request needs more than a single step, then call it again to mark items in_progress / completed as you go. Skip it for simple questions or greetings.

tools.name: write_file
tools.description: Write a file to your virtual scratchpad so intermediate findings survive across steps (beyond the context window). Use it to stash research notes or partial work.

tools.name: read_file
tools.description: Read a file back from your virtual scratchpad by path.

tools.name: edit_file
tools.description: Edit a scratchpad file in place: replace the first occurrence of old_string with new_string (append new_string if old_string is empty).

tools.name: ls
tools.description: List the files currently in your virtual scratchpad.

tools.name: task
tools.description: Delegate a focused sub-task to a sub-agent that runs with its own isolated context and tools, and returns only its final result — so the gathering never clutters your own context. Use it for self-contained research.

Use it to ground answers about those subjects. Do NOT use it for real-world facts, current events, news, sports, people, prices, or anything outside AI engineering — for those, use `web_search`. This corpus always returns its closest passages even when nothing is truly relevant, so only call it on-topic.

data.system:
You are Agent Simulator. AI Agent Simulator — explore how an agent works.

Platform guardrails (apply to every agent):
- Be helpful, accurate and concise.
- When you used retrieved context or a tool result, rely on it and say so; do not invent facts.
- If you do not have enough information, say so plainly rather than guess.
- Refuse to help with illegal, harmful, deceptive or unsafe requests.
- Prefer plain prose; use bullet lists only when the user asks for them or the content is genuinely a list.


You are the assistant inside an "AI Agent Simulator", a teaching tool that visualizes how an agentic application works.

You have tools available and you decide, on your own, whether and when to call them:
- `search_knowledge_base` is your PRIMARY knowledge tool. For any question about a concept, how something works, a comparison, or anything that could be documented, you MUST call `search_knowledge_base` first and ground your answer in what it returns — do not answer such questions from memory alone.
- For arithmetic or numeric calculations, call the `calculator` tool instead of computing in your head.
- For the current date or time, call `current_time`.
- `kb_lookup` only returns a single canned one-line glossary string for a few basic terms; prefer `search_knowledge_base` for anything more than a trivial one-word definition.


You are running as a DeepAgent — you handle tasks deliberately, not in one reflex step. You MUST follow this workflow for every user request that is an actual task or question (the ONLY exception is a bare greeting or social pleasantry with no task — then just reply):

1. PLAN FIRST. Before doing anything else, call `write_todos` to lay out an ordered plan (2–5 concrete steps). Do this even for seemingly simple questions — it is how you work.
2. WORK THE PLAN. Execute the steps. As you start a step call `write_todos` again to mark it `in_progress`, and after finishing it mark it `completed`, so your plan always reflects reality. Keep exactly one item `in_progress` at a time.
3. USE YOUR SCRATCHPAD. Use `write_file` / `read_file` / `edit_file` / `ls` to store intermediate findings (e.g. write research notes to `research.md`) so your work survives across steps instead of being lost to the context window.
4. DELEGATE RESEARCH. For self-contained investigation, call `task` to hand it to a sub-agent that runs with its own isolated context and returns only its result — keeping your own context clean.
5. FINISH. When every todo is `completed`, synthesise the final answer for the user from your plan, files and sub-agent results.

Always start with `write_todos`. A response that skips planning is a failure of your role.


You also have access to these reusable Skills. When one fits the user's request, call the `load_skill` tool with its exact name to load its full instructions, then follow them:
- explicar-para-iniciante: Use quando o usuário pedir uma explicação simples, 'para iniciante', 'sem jargão' ou 'como se eu tivesse 5 anos'.
- glossario-ao-final: Use quando o usuário pedir um glossário ou quando a resposta usar termos técnicos.
- resumo-em-bullets: Use quando o usuário pedir um resumo, 'em tópicos' ou bullet points.

chunks.text:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation is a pattern that grounds a language model in
external knowledge. Instead of relying only on what the model memorized during
training, the system retrieves relevant documents at query time and places them
in the prompt as context. This reduces hallucinations and lets the model answer
questions about private or recent data it never saw during training.

chunks.text:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation is a pattern that grounds a language model in
external knowledge. Instead of relying only on what the model memorized during
training, the system retrieves relevant documents at query time and places them
in the prompt as context. This reduces hallucinations and lets the model answer

chunks.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation is a pattern that grounds a…

chunks.text:
A typical RAG pipeline has two phases. The offline **ingestion** phase loads
source documents, splits them into chunks, computes an embedding vector for each
chunk, and stores those vectors in a vector database. The online **retrieval**
phase embeds the user's query with the same model, performs a nearest-neighbor
search to find the most similar chunks, and injects the top matches into the
prompt sent to the LLM.

data.context:
[rag.md] Retrieval-Augmented Generation is a pattern that grounds a language model in
external knowledge. Instead of relying only on what the model memorized during
training, the system retrieves relevant documents at query time and places them
in the prompt as context. This reduces hallucinations and lets the model answer
questions about private or recent data it never saw during training.

[rag.md] A typical RAG pipeline has two phases. The offline **ingestion** phase loads
source documents, splits them into chunks, computes an embedding vector for each
chunk, and stores those vectors in a vector database. The online **retrieval**
phase embeds the user's query with the same model, performs a nearest-neighbor
search to find the most similar chunks, and injects the top matches into the
prompt sent to the LLM.

You are Agent Simulator. AI Agent Simulator — explore how an agent works.

Platform guardrails (apply to every agent):
- Be helpful, accurate and concise.
- When you used retrieved context or a tool result, rely on it and say so; do not invent facts.
- If you do not have enough information, say so plainly rather than guess.
- Refuse to help with illegal, harmful, deceptive or unsafe requests.
- Prefer plain prose; use bullet lists only when the user asks for them or the content is genuinely a list.


You are the assistant inside an "AI Agent Simulator", a teaching tool that visualizes how an agentic application works.

You have tools available and you decide, on your own, whether and when to call them:
- `search_knowledge_base` is your PRIMARY knowledge tool. For any question about a concept, how something works, a comparison, or anything that could be documented, you MUST call `search_knowledge_base` first and ground your answer in what it returns — do not answer such questions from memory alone.
- For arithmetic or numeric calculations, call the `calculator` tool instead of computing in your head.
- For the current date or time, call `current_time`.
- `kb_lookup` only returns a single canned one-line glossary string for a few basic terms; prefer `search_knowledge_base` for anything more than a trivial one-word definition.


You are running as a DeepAgent — you handle tasks deliberately, not in one reflex step. You MUST follow this workflow for every user request that is an actual task or question (the ONLY exception is a bare greeting or social pleasantry with no task — then just reply):

1. PLAN FIRST. Before doing anything else, call `write_todos` to lay out an ordered plan (2–5 concrete steps). Do this even for seemingly simple questions — it is how you work.
2. WORK THE PLAN. Execute the steps. As you start a step call `write_todos` again to mark it `in_progress`, and after finishing it mark it `completed`, so your plan always reflects reality. Keep exactly one item `in_progress` at a time.
3. USE YOUR SCRATCHPAD. Use `write_file` / `read_file` / `edit_file` / `ls` to store intermediate findings (e.g. write research notes to `research.md`) so your work survives across steps instead of being lost to the context window.
4. DELEGATE RESEARCH. For self-contained investigation, call `task` to hand it to a sub-agent that runs with its own isolated context and returns only its result — keeping your own context clean.
5. FINISH. When every todo is `completed`, synthesise the final answer for the user from your plan, files and sub-agent results.

Always start with `write_todos`. A response that skips planning is a failure of your role.


You also have access to these reusable Skills. When one fits the user's request, call the `load_skill` tool with its exact name to load its full instructions, then follow them:
- explicar-para-iniciante: Use quando o usuário pedir uma explicação simples, 'para iniciante', 'sem jargão' ou 'como se eu tivesse 5 anos'.
- glossario-ao-final: Use quando o usuário pedir um glossário ou quando a resposta usar termos técnicos.
- resumo-em-bullets: Use quando o usuário pedir um resumo, 'em tópicos' ou bullet points.

messages.content: What is RAG and how does retrieval work?

data.context:
[rag.md] Retrieval-Augmented Generation is a pattern that grounds a language model in
external knowledge. Instead of relying only on what the model memorized during
training, the system retrieves relevant documents at query time and places them
in the prompt as context. This reduces hallucinations and lets the model answer
questions about private or recent data it never saw during training.

[rag.md] A typical RAG pipeline has two phases. The offline **ingestion** phase loads
source documents, splits them into chunks, computes an embedding vector for each
chunk, and stores those vectors in a vector database. The online **retrieval**
phase embeds the user's query with the same model, performs a nearest-neighbor
search to find the most similar chunks, and injects the top matches into the
prompt sent to the LLM.

args.content:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a pattern that grounds a language model in external knowledge. Instead of relying only on what the model memorized during training, the system retrieves relevant documents at query time and places them in the prompt as context. This approach reduces hallucinations and enables the model to answer questions about private or recent data it never saw during training.

The RAG pipeline typically has two phases:
1. Offline ingestion phase: Source documents are loaded, split into chunks, and an embedding vector is computed for each chunk. These vectors are stored in a vector database.
2. Online retrieval phase: The user's query is embedded with the same model, and a nearest-neighbor search is performed to find the most similar chunks. The top matching chunks are then injected into the prompt sent to the language model (LLM).

This retrieval process allows the language model to access up-to-date and relevant information dynamically, improving accuracy and relevance in its responses.

The RAG pipeline typically has two phases:
1. Offline ingestion phase: Source documents are loaded, split into chunks, and an embedding vector is computed for each chunk. These vectors are stored in a vector database.
2. Online retrieval phase: The user's query is embedded with the same model, and a nearest-neighbor search is performed to find the most similar chunks. The top matching chunks are then injected into the prompt sent to the language model (LLM).

This retrieval process allows the language model to access up-to-date and relevant information dynamically, improving accuracy and relevance in its responses.

messages.content: What is RAG and how does retrieval work?

data.context:
[rag.md] Retrieval-Augmented Generation is a pattern that grounds a language model in
external knowledge. Instead of relying only on what the model memorized during
training, the system retrieves relevant documents at query time and places them
in the prompt as context. This reduces hallucinations and lets the model answer
questions about private or recent data it never saw during training.

[rag.md] A typical RAG pipeline has two phases. The offline **ingestion** phase loads
source documents, splits them into chunks, computes an embedding vector for each
chunk, and stores those vectors in a vector database. The online **retrieval**
phase embeds the user's query with the same model, performs a nearest-neighbor
search to find the most similar chunks, and injects the top matches into the
prompt sent to the LLM.

data.answer:
RAG, or Retrieval-Augmented Generation, is a technique that enhances a language model by grounding it in external knowledge. Instead of relying solely on what the model learned during training, RAG retrieves relevant documents at the time of a query and includes them as context in the prompt. This helps reduce hallucinations and allows the model to answer questions about private or recent information it hasn't seen before.

The RAG process has two main phases:

1. Offline ingestion: Source documents are loaded, split into chunks, and each chunk is converted into an embedding vector. These vectors are stored in a vector database.
2. Online retrieval: When a user submits a query, it is embedded using the same model, and a nearest-neighbor search finds the most similar document chunks. The top matches are then added to the prompt sent to the language model.

This retrieval mechanism enables the language model to dynamically access up-to-date and relevant information, improving the accuracy and relevance of its responses.

events.label: Returning the answer to the user

data.answer:
RAG, or Retrieval-Augmented Generation, is a technique that enhances a language model by grounding it in external knowledge. Instead of relying solely on what the model learned during training, RAG retrieves relevant documents at the time of a query and includes them as context in the prompt. This helps reduce hallucinations and allows the model to answer questions about private or recent information it hasn't seen before.

The RAG process has two main phases:

1. Offline ingestion: Source documents are loaded, split into chunks, and each chunk is converted into an embedding vector. These vectors are stored in a vector database.
2. Online retrieval: When a user submits a query, it is embedded using the same model, and a nearest-neighbor search finds the most similar document chunks. The top matches are then added to the prompt sent to the language model.

This retrieval mechanism enables the language model to dynamically access up-to-date and relevant information, improving the accuracy and relevance of its responses.
